[[RunningHead]]

Dependent and Autonomous Interpersonal Styles and Level of Differentiation among Male Patients with Eating Disorders

Lily Rothschild-Yakar PHD , Yael Bashan-Levi MA , Itshak Vorgaft MD , Eitan Gur MD and Daniel Stein MD

Daniel Stein

199 Aba Khoushy Avenue, Mount Carmel, Haifa 3498838, Israel. E-mail: lilyrot01@gmail.com

Ziv Medical Center, Zefat, Israel

Sheba Medical Center, Tel Hashomer, Israel

Safra Children's Hospital, Sheba Medical Center, Tel Hashomer, Israel and Tel-Aviv University

Daniel Stein  

eating disorders in men, dependency, autonomy, differentiation

This study examined whether difficulties in maintaining a balance between the personality variables of relatedness (dependency) and self-definition (autonomy) together with a low level of differentiation would distinguish male patients with eating disorders (EDs) from controls. The study is based on Blatt's dual model of personality configurations: relatedness (anaclitic) versus self-definition (introjective). It is further based on conceptualizations of differentiation put forward by Mahler (1968); Blatt and Luyten (2009), and by Bowen (1978). In the study we compared 25 male patients between the ages of 14–24 years hospitalized because of EDs to 25 non-ED male controls matched for age. We found that male patients with EDs exhibited elevated levels of both dependency and autonomy and a lower level of differentiation compared to non-ED controls. Moreover, these personality characteristics were significantly associated with elevated ED symptomatology, above and beyond the effect of emotional distress. These findings highlight the potential role played by these personality attributes in the predisposition to develop and sustain an ED, especially the ability to differentiate one's own mental experiences from those of others.

This article was published Online First September 21, 2015.

responseSet.title: Correction to Trotter and Harlem (2016)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Correction to Trotter and Harlem (2016)

In the book review of “Cyclical Psychodynamics and the Contextual Self” by Amber M. Trotter, and Andrew Harlem (Psychoanalytic Psychology, 2016, Vol 33, No.3, pp. 525-529. http://dx.doi.org/10.1037/pap0000041), there was an error in the 10th paragraph. The paragraph should have read as follows:

“While Part 1 of Cyclical Psychodynamics lays out its author's conception of psychoanalysis, the second half (Part 2) is dedicated to drawing out its implications for the broader social world. It is here that the book comes most fully to life. Wachtel argues that psychoanalysis has much to contribute to our understanding of sociopolitical life, turning his attention most squarely to the issues of race, class, greed, and the “social construction of desire.” Extending psychoanalytic thinking into these realms is, for Wachtel, not simply a matter of personal passion; rather, he views it as a responsibility, a necessity. “Neurotic misery,” he writes, “is not the only treatable source of suffering that can be subtracted from the sum of everyday unhappiness that we mere mortals can avoid” (p. 195).

responseSet.title: The Import of Feeling in the Organization of Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Import of Feeling in the Organization of Mind

Margaret Browning PHD

Margaret Browning

190 Calle Girasol, Isabela, Puerto Rico 00662. E-mail: mmastersb@gmail.com

Margaret Browning  

feeling, mind, symbol, agency, intentionality

This article delineates from a developmental perspective the import of feeling in the organization of mind. The argument is framed within a formulation of the concepts of agency and intentionality examined in their ongoing transformation from nonsymbolic to symbolic modes as these modes are defined. The author builds upon the neuroscientific work of Jaak Panksepp and the philosophical psychology of Susanne Langer and completes the developmental argument by making use of Marcia Cavell's book The Psychoanalytic Mind: From Freud to Philosophy. From the earliest days of infancy, the child's affective agency, operative from the deepest levels of nonconscious functioning, includes a rudimentary state of sentience. As argued here, this rudimentary state defines consciousness, a state of continuously shifting feeling arising out of nonconscious complexity. This fundamental sentience, this primary state of consciousness, is the child's capacity to feel. Growing up in a cultural environment built upon symbolic transformations of feeling, the child gradually acquires the capacity to become a symbol user herself and an agent whose intentions can be intersubjectively shared.

Michael O'loughlin

130 Harvey Hall, Adelphi University, Garden City NY 11530. E-mail: michaeloloughlinphd@gmail.com

Michael O'loughlin  

Barnaby Barratt opens his provocative book with a broadside against all of the major contemporary schools of psychoanalysis. He worries that, far from an indicator of health, the profusion of psychoanalytic schools has produced “theoretical bedlam” and such a large tent that “the rubric of ‘psychoanalysis’ has become virtually meaningless” (p. xiv). Barratt argues for a return of psychoanalysis to its fundamental Freudian roots. He is scornful of the orthodoxies of analytic training institutes that, all too often, are exclusively concerned with externalities (e.g., frequency of session, use of the couch), and with indoctrination of candidates into an unquestioning allegiance to authority. This, he argues, is antithetical to a genuinely psychoanalytic turn of mind. Barratt is concerned with the ossification of the field and with a legendarily disputatious culture in which analysts of different persuasions “become rabidly condescending or dismissive toward each other” (p. 8). Ironically, Barratt, himself, is an equal opportunity critic, and none of the existing schools of psychoanalysis escapes his wrath. He sums up his critique thus:

Indeed, the history of the psychoanalytic movement through the 20th century provides an appalling spectacle of in-groups and tightly knit clubs fighting with each other, of internecine rivalries, backbiting and name-calling that is only thinly veiled in professional courtesy, of rampant authoritarianism, of politically motivated expulsions and the formation of counter-organizations, and thus of proliferative organizational splitting (p. 5).

Barratt's book is a call for a return to a “vivacious engagement” with the kind of inquiry that ought to constitute the core of psychoanalysis. Sadly, while Barratt calls for a return to the foundational ideas of Freudian psychoanalysis, he acknowledges that Freud's constitution of the Secret Committee in 1912 to police doctrinal orthodoxy and ensure his legacy, laid the groundwork for the development of psychoanalysis as a faith to be policed, rather than as a vigorous intellectual movement that constantly reinvented itself. Barratt maintains a Pollyannaish view of other scientific disciplines, and regrets that psychoanalysis lacks similar theoretical vigor and capacity for questioning. Changing Encounters with the other: A Focus Group Study on the Process of Change in a Therapeutic Community

Virginie Debaere MA , Stijn Vanheule PHD , Kaatje Van Roy MD MA , Reitske Meganck PHD , Ruth Inslegers PHD and Martie Mol MA

Martie Mol

H. Dunantlaan 2, B-9000 Ghent, Belgium. E-mail: Virginie.Debaere@UGent.be

Antwerp, Belgium

Martie Mol  

first-person perspectives, process research, qualitative research, therapeutic community

Democratic therapeutic communities (TCs) are long-term group programs that address severely ingrained clinical populations. These psychodynamically informed social environments can facilitate improvement in people suffering from personality pathology. However, the TCs' working principle is not well documented, which threatens its continued existence. To gain further insight into how TCs work, this study explores former TC residents' perspectives on their treatment, its outcome, and the process of change they underwent. Four steps that might explain this process were identified through focus group interviews (using qualitative analyses) with 24 former residents of a Belgian democratic TC: (a) I encounter a safe, caring, and challenging Other, (b) I unfold my particular way of interacting with the Other, (c) I am confronted with the Otherness in me, and (d) I live an Other life. This fourth step refers to the reported treatment outcome that consists of 3 main changes: (a) residents became more resilient and capable of coping with their problems, (b) residents became more involved in pleasant social relations, and (c) residents developed the capacity to make choices in their own lives. We recommend a reappraisal of social psychiatry, emphasizing the social aspect of human life, psychopathology and its treatment. Limitations of the study and suggestions for further research are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Cyclical Psychodynamics and the Contextual Self, by Paul L. Wachtel, New York, NY: Routledge, 2014, 244 pp., ISBN: 9780415713955 (Paperback), $54.95

Amber Trotter MA and Andrew Harlem PHD

Andrew Harlem

3610 Sacramento Street, San Francisco, CA 94118. E-mail: adharlem@comcast.net

Andrew Harlem  

Paul Wachtel has been thinking and writing about psychoanalysis for nearly 50 years. And, thankfully for us, he has resisted conformity. Wachtel championed a contextual, postmodern conception of the self before it was fashionable, and his incisive critiques of psychoanalytic insularity have helped expand the margins of our discipline. Although his integrative approach to psychotherapy reflects many strands of thinking that have come to be associated with the broader relational turn, his vision for psychoanalysis has its own distinctive incubation. This has been most evident in his unflinching pursuit of breadth, his deep appreciation for science and ethics, his insistence that psychoanalysis dialogue with other disciplines, and his consideration of the social world when imagining the psychoanalytic subject.

Cyclical Psychodynamics and the Contextual Self: The Inner World, the Intimate World, and the World of Culture and Society, Wachtel's latest volume, synthesizes ideas cured over decades. The title alone signals the striking ambition of this project—one that its author carries out with characteristic pragmatism and clarity, weaving together culture and psyche in ways that illuminate both clinical practice and our contemporary cultural landscape. Cyclical Psychodynamics is readily accessible without foregoing complexity. In addition, its author's reflections on his long involvement with the psychoanalytic community give the book a winning historical grounding.

The first half of Cyclical Psychodynamics orients the reader to Wachtel's particular psychoanalytic vision. At the heart of his “cyclical” theory is the conviction that our inner (private, subjective), intimate (primary relationships), and sociocultural worlds constantly interpenetrate and shape each other. Although lines of influence extend in every direction, Wachtel emphasizes the circular fashion in which we tend to elicit through precisely the responses from the world we most fear. He writes, “The tragedy of psychopathology lies in the excruciating (if unwitting) skill with which the suffering individual renovates those structures [that cause one's suffering] again and again throughout life” (p. 79).

Reading French Psychoanalysis, Edited by Dana Birksted-Breen, Sara Flanders, and Alain Gibeault, London, United Kingdom: Routledge, 2010, 817 pp., $64.95.

Paul Verhaeghe PHD , Stijn Vanheule PHD , Filip Geerardyn PHD , Reitske Meganck PHD and Eline Trenson Msc

Eline Trenson

Henri Dunantlaan 2, BE-9000 Ghent, Belgium. E-mail: Paul.Verhaeghe@UGent.be

Eline Trenson  

Needless to say that this is a voluminous book. As a matter of fact, it contains seven books, covering six different topics and a 53-page general introduction. Each section of the book is headed by an introductory chapter written by Alain Gibeault and followed by a selection of originally French papers translated into English. On the other hand, the book is not voluminous enough because the largest part of what is announced by the title (Reading French Psychoanalysis) is lacking, that is, Lacanian psychoanalysis. In the General Introduction we learn that the editors have concentrated on the production of two societies that belong to the International Psychoanalytical Society. There is nothing wrong with that, except that to present this as French psychoanalysis is a gross overstatement, especially in a series that wants to be teaching. This is all the more deplorable because two of the three editors note that “the single most important influence marking the unique development of French psychoanalysis has been Lacan's original and revolutionary intrusion into the psychoanalytic culture (…)” and “[h]is direct influence appears to have been underplayed by French psychoanalysts belonging to the IPA” (pp. 2-3).

Usually, introductions are boring and superfluous. This one is not; on the contrary, it presents the reader—especially the Anglo-Saxon reader—with a balanced and informed view on the differences and resemblances of French and Anglo-Saxon psychoanalysis. By and large, with their general introduction, Dana Birksted-Breen and Sara Flanders demonstrate their insight into the conceptual history of French Psychoanalysis and the differences with the Anglo-Saxon tradition: the mixture with philosophy, the role of the Phallus and the Other, the idea of après-coup, the absence of training analysis, the importance of the drives (instead of instincts) and their ever difficult representation, psychosomatics and pensée operatoire, and of course, the psychoanalytic process and the setting with the typically French focus on both silence and speech.

Yianna Ioannou  

Mended by the Muse: Creative Transformations of Trauma by Sophia Richman (2014) is at once a deeply personal account and a contemporary psychoanalytic meditation on trauma. The book is concerned with “the art of trauma,” the creative process arising out of the effort to cope with traumatic experiences. Deftly moving between theory, practice, and personal experience, Richman explores the different forms that trauma takes across generations and between individuals, but also the fluctuating and shifting nature of individual experience within each person, across space and time, in a manner that respects and highlights the complex ways in which the human mind can experience itself. Stemming from the relational psychoanalytic tradition, the book expands the function of dissociation, which is commonly perceived as a pathological hallmark of trauma, and illuminates instead its creative potential. According to Richman, dissociated states that are summoned with the intent and purpose of engaging in creative pursuits may, in fact, allow the trauma survivor to repair the damaged link to the world. The imaginary figure of the muse accompanies the survivor in this process, and serves a mothering function that helps sustain the survivor's need to communicate. Richman's theoretical formulations are rooted in her personal history of transforming trauma through artistic creation, and draw on her clinical experiences with traumatized patients who turn to the arts as a way of working through, as well as the experience of artists who attest to the therapeutic value of their creative process.

True to its emphasis on creativity, the book is itself a creative synthesis of various modes of writing, which range from a theoretical review of psychoanalytic literature on creativity, to lively renderings of clinical material, intimate accounts of autobiographical and biographical narratives, interviews with artists, a case study of Jung's creativity, reflections on mortality, a self-reflection on the clinical impact of publicly disclosing a traumatic autobiography, and revelations of personal and family experiences, most notable among which is the story of Richman's daughter, Lina, who turned to music as a way of coping with a debilitating chronic illness. responseSet.title: Erotomania According to Lars von Trier
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Erotomania According to Lars von Trier

Andrea Sabbadini and C. Psychol

C. Psychol

British Psychoanalytical Society, London, UK
38 Berkeley Road, London N8 8RU, United Kingdom. E-mail: a.sabbadini@gmail.com

C. Psychol  

addictive behavior, erotomania, film Nymphomaniac, Lars von Trier, repetition compulsion

The recent 4-hr-long film Nymphomaniac (2013), written and directed by the controversial Danish director Lars von Trier, provides here an opportunity for psychoanalytically informed reflections on some of the issues presented by the film and by its main 2 characters. Joe is a middle-aged woman who, in the course of recovering from an aggressive attack which had left her badly injured, recounts in flashback narrative her lifelong journey through compulsory, and mostly unsatisfactory, sexual experiences in their multiple variations on the perverse spectrum. Seligman is the older man who, having rescued her and invited her to his home, takes on a paternal and pseudotherapeutic role by listening sympathetically to her stories. He genuinely attempts to comprehend and justify them, but being himself addicted to books while having an only limited range of life experiences, he can only relate them to all sort of cultural phenomena—from such fields as numerology, history of religion, literary criticism, visual art, and musicology. The psychological mechanism of repetition compulsion as a major component of addictive behaviors is presented here as central to the understanding of Joe's painful vicissitudes. Her compulsive search for the ultimate sensual gratification, but without emotional involvement or interpersonal commitment on her part, and indeed with no concern for the suffering caused to those she sexually abuses, is seen here as a major contribution to Joe's profound sense of loneliness.

[[RunningHead]]

Moving Images: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Film, by Andrea Sabbadini (New Library of Psychoanalysis) London and New York: Routledge, 2014, 140 pp. (ISBN 978-0415736121, US $49.95 Paperback)

Krin Gabbard PHD

Krin Gabbard

403 West 115th Street, Apartment 51, New York, NY 10025. E-mail: krin.gabbard@stonybrook.edu

Krin Gabbard  

Andrea Sabbadini, a psychoanalyst practicing in London, has written a short but stimulating survey of some films that have, to paraphrase the title of this volume, “moved” him. He freely admits that his choices are highly personal and hence arbitrary. He also disavows any attempt to provide “a systematic, scholarly approach to film criticism” and “any new general theoretical model for the interpretation of films” (p. xvii). For better or worse, Sabbadini is just as eclectic and unsystematic as he applies different psychoanalytic tools to individual films.

Sadly, some of the films that the author writes about are so obscure that most mental health professionals and even many in the film studies community will never have heard of them. As of this writing, the most obscure item, the Bulgarian film Mila from Mars (2004), is not available on DVD, and another, the Swedish film Lilya-4-Ever (2002), is only available in a format incompatible with most American DVD-players. In spite of his rarefied, international aesthetic, Sabbadini does, however, have much in common with the many psychoanalytically minded film buffs who are fascinated by Alfred Hitchcock. The book offers short sections on Spellbound (1945), Rear Window (1954), and Vertigo (1958). Otherwise, most of the films that Sabbadini writes about are European and belong to what film scholars would call “the art cinema.” In other words, films that cry out for explanation.

Moving Images is broken into six chapters, each one devoting several pages to three, four, or five individual films. The strengths and weaknesses of Sabbadini's approach are apparent in the first chapter, “A Young Profession: Films on Psychoanalysis.” This chapter is built around four films from four different decades: Secrets of a Soul (1926), directed during the heady days of German expressionist cinema by G.W. Pabst; the British film Nineteen Nineteen (1985), directed by Hugh Brody; Il Postino (1994), shot in Italy and directed by Michael Radford; and Hitchcock's Spellbound. Each of these films provides us with a snapshot of how the profession of psychoanalysis was regarded in its moment. In the 1920s, the German director Pabst was enthralled by Freud's increasingly influential ideas and put them to use as the solution to a mystery story. [[RunningHead]]

The Rupture of Serenity: External Intrusions and Psychoanalytic Technique, by Aisha Abassi, MD, London, England: Karnac, 2014, 154 pp., $37.95.

Axel Hoffer MD

Axel Hoffer

14 Welland Road, Brookline, MA 02445, Boston, MA. E-mail: axhoffer@hms.harvard.edu

Axel Hoffer  

Rarely is a psychoanalytic book a page-turner; this one is. What makes this small volume so inviting to the reader? Unlike the familiar psychoanalytic text, this book is immediately personal, not abstract, not at a distance. By the time we have turned the last page, we feel we know Dr. Abassi personally. I think it is because of the author's willingness to share directly with the reader her vulnerability and humanity. The reader is immediately drawn into her analytic life, with her personal conflicts, her imperfections, and her suffering. She steadfastly maintains her consistent attention to the needs of her patient. We are given access to how she resolves, by effective self-analysis, the origins of her countertransferences.

The ruptures of the serenity of her analytic practice are dramatic and profound. Dr. Abassi includes the highly personal intrusions on the serenity of her practice. These include having to share with her patients her problems with infertility and the patients learning she has a transgender child. She vividly describes the impact of 9/11 on her and on several of her patients. She does not shy away from discussing the complexity of a Muslim analyst analyzing a Jewish patient. Furthermore, challenged and puzzled by a patient who insists on bringing a tape recorder to each session, she brings that issue into a lengthy and meaningful analysis of this behavior. Finally, I found particularly challenging what follows after she gives her patient access to her telephone in an apparent emergency. The patient intrudes on her privacy and then confesses it. With these examples, she teaches the reader how to analyze what we usually call “acting out” into the analytic process. Less dramatic, but no less fascinating, are her analyses of her observations of the behaviors of her patients in the waiting room and how she skillfully makes observations that can be talked about and analyzed.

The way Dr. Abassi deals with her patients' often quite sadistic anger at her, their doubts about her capacity to understand them, and their contempt for her serves as a model of how someone well analyzed usefully first works through for herself the countertransference and then makes use of it for the patient's benefit. Her careful use of measured self-revelations, always for the patient's benefit, is remarkable.

[[RunningHead]]

The Mirror Paradigm: Assessing the Embodied Self in the Context of Abuse

Karin Ensink PHD , Nicolas Berthelot PHD , Marko Biberdzic BA and Lina Normandin PHD

Lina Normandin

2325 rue des Bibliothèques, Québec City, QC G1V 0A6. E-mail: Karin.Ensink@psy.ulaval.ca

Lina Normandin  

development of the self, embodied self, childhood sexual abuse, mirror paradigm

Childhood sexual abuse is a known risk factor for the development of mental health problems in adolescence and adulthood. However, the developmental mechanisms are not well understood. We hypothesized that sexual abuse has an early impact on the development of the self, which plays a key role in adaptation following the trauma. We posit that many of the negative impacts of abuse on the developing self affect sensory-motor states, as well as one's own body experience, and remain mostly out of cognitive awareness. This study presents a new method to assess the impact of sexual abuse on children's embodied self-experience in a sample of 68 children aged 5-13 (43 children with histories of sexual abuse and 25 nonabused children). A coding procedure was developed for use with the mirror paradigm (MP) to assess 4 dimensions of children's embodied self-experience (MP-CESE). The MP consists of a semistructured interview during which the child is requested to answer questions while looking at his image in a vertical mirror. Findings indicate that the embodied self-experiences of children with histories of sexual abuse were significantly more negative than those of nonabused controls. Embodied self-experience scores as measured with the MP-CESE correlated significantly with parent reports of internalized behaviors, externalized behaviors, dissociation and sexualized behaviors, as well as teacher's reports of children's externalizing behaviors. This suggests that the MP-CESE is a reliable measure that provides access to disturbances of the embodied self in school-age children, which cut across numerous behavioral problems.

Listening, Learning, and Development in Psychoanalytic Supervision: A Self Psychology Perspective

C. Watkins PHD

C. Watkins

1155 Union Circle #311280, Denton, Texas. E-mail: watkinsc@unt.edu

C. Watkins  

psychoanalytic, supervision, therapist development, practice self, self psychology

In this article, I would like to consider the matter of development in psychoanalytic supervision and how supervisees learn and grow across supervisory space. Although long considered fundamentally developmental in nature, the process of psychoanalytic supervision has not been specifically examined and explicated from a developmental learning perspective. I propose here some ways in which a developmental learning view could complement a psychoanalytic view of supervision. Blending therapist/supervisee developmental conceptualization (e.g., Ronnestad & Skovholt, 2013; Skovholt, 2012; Stoltenberg & McNeill, 2010) with a self psychology focus on the therapist self, I take up 3 issues: (a) a vision of learning in supervision; (b) the evolving sense of practice self during the supervisory process; and (c) how subject-centered and other-centered listening perspectives contribute developmentally to the supervision experience. My perspective is informed by and grounded in the foundational assumption that: Psychoanalytic supervision is a developmentally anchored educational experience in which the supervisory relationship serves as the crucible of supervisee change.

Clinical Implications of the Psychoanalyst's Life Experience: When the Personal Becomes Professional, edited by Stephen Kuchuk, New York, NY: Routledge, 2014, + 254 pp., $45.37 (paperback)

Patrick Casement MA

Patrick Casement

122 Mansfield Road, London NW3 2 JB, UK. E-mail: casement.patrick@gmail.com

Patrick Casement  

I do not think that there can be a better description of this book than that which Kuchuk writes in his introduction:

In the following pages, established psychoanalytic writers and newer contributors come together to address the phenomena of the analyst's personal life and psychology. Each author explores pivotal childhood and adult life events and crises that have contributed to personality formation, personal and professional functioning, choices of theoretical positions, and clinical technique. (p. xviii).

The book is a treasure trove that can only be fully appreciated by studying it. From a wide range of experiences, the authors have applied themselves to the invitation, to examine the ways in which their life experiences had shaped how they are, in themselves and in their approach to their clinical work. They have also, many of them, shared with the reader some of their own struggles with the issues of self-exposure—or remaining hidden. How might this affect their work with patients? How might their patients' transference experience of the analyst be colored by such exposure, which it surely will be? Might this restrict the freedom to work meaningfully in the transference? Might this become impossible, or might it—in some way—be enhanced by this?

There is a repeating theme, throughout this book, around various kinds of “coming out.” Not only do we hear of gay and lesbian therapists struggling with this, unsure of how this might be received by patients and/or by colleagues, but we also hear some careful reflections upon how exposure through publication could also impinge upon the therapists' ongoing work with patients, or with patients yet to come.

How concerned should we be about these kinds of self-exposure? In one sense, we now live in quite a different world from that of our analytic forebears. It has always been a myth that the analyst could maintain clinical anonymity, and this is all the more impossible now that there are so many other ways by which patients can find out about their analysts, with the help of Google and other forms of exploration outside of the consulting room. But all of that is different from what patients can discern from their own more direct observation of their analysts.

Couple Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy as the Treatment of Choice: Indications, Challenges and Benefits

Berta Aznar-Martínez PHD , Carles Pérez-Testor PHD MD , Montserat Davins PHD and Inés Aramburu PHD

Inés Aramburu

FPCEE Blanquerna. C/Císter 34. 08022. Barcelona, Spain. E-mail: bertaam@blanquerna.url.edu

Inés Aramburu  

couple psychotherapy, therapeutic relationship, transference, countertransference, psychoanalysis, conjoint treatment

Including couple treatment in psychoanalysis has required the setting of new parameters beyond the classical psychoanalytical setting, in which the treatment is individual. This article aims to define the clinical criteria for, and benefits of, recommending couple treatment rather than individual psychoanalysis or psychotherapy, and to identify the challenges and demands that this has entailed for psychoanalysis, from the standpoint of the analysis itself and also that of the therapeutic relationship. Couple therapy is a very complex endeavor since a host of factors must be borne in mind. The present paper discusses the specific features of these factors and how they influence the diverse mechanisms in the analytical relationship. A clinical vignette is included in order to demonstrate the mechanisms that influence therapeutic work in couple psychoanalytic treatment.

It Takes Two to Tango: Introduction to the Special Issue on Psychoanalysis and the Humanities

Elliot Jurist PHD PHD and Spyros Orfanos PHD

Spyros Orfanos

NAC 8/109, New York, NY 10031. E-mail: ejurist5@gmail.com

Spyros Orfanos  

psychoanalysis, the humanities, the two cultures, clinical psychology

The Introduction describes our reasons for undertaking a special issue on psychoanalysis and the humanities and offers reflections on the relation between the two fields. In particular, we discuss the implications of the diminished importance of these two fields within our culture, and will also consider this in relation to recent developments in clinical psychology. We move on to briefly characterize the thirteen articles contained in this issue, some of which have a general focus on psychoanalysis and the humanities, and some of which have a specific focus, such as history, philosophy, painting, literature, film, photography, performance, music and poetry.

On a Culturally Humble Psychoanalytic Supervision Perspective: Creating the Cultural Third

C. Watkins and Joshua Hook

Joshua Hook

1155 Union Circle #311280, Denton, TX 76203-5017. E-mail: watkinsc@unt.edu

Joshua Hook  

cultural humility, supervision, psychoanalytic, psychodynamic, diversity

What is the role of cultural humility in psychoanalytic supervision? In this article, we address that question. While culture has been recognized as central to supervision practice (e.g., Tummala-Narra, 2004), the psychoanalytic supervision literature remains highly limited in addressing issues related to culture and diversity. In what follows, we present a psychoanalytic supervision perspective that is anchored by the construct of cultural humility. Cultural humility is defined, 10 conceptual/practice guideposts of a culturally humble supervision view are proposed, 2 supervision case examples are described, and some supervision/cultural humility research hypotheses are proposed for possible empirical study. Teaching supervisees about and modeling cultural humility is considered to be preparatory education for the creation of a cultural third. Realizing cultural humility via supervisory interaction involves the opening of a supervisor-supervisee third space whereby cultural meanings and experiences are welcomed and can be explored, examined, and experienced anew. This supervision cultural third ideally becomes a prototype for the supervisee/patient's own creation of a treatment cultural third.

responseSet.title: The Player and the Game: Compulsion, Relation, and Potential Space in Video Games
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Player and the Game: Compulsion, Relation, and Potential Space in Video Games

Alexander Kriss PHD

Alexander Kriss

531 Bergen St #3R, Brooklyn, NY. E-mail: alexkriss@gmail.com

Alexander Kriss  

playing, potential space, video games

For years, video games have been anecdotally associated with psychopathology, despite their increasing popularity among the general public and the lack of empirical evidence to support such a link. In this article, the author argues that games are more than symptoms and that game worlds can act as a kind of potential space (Winnicott, 1971/2005) not fully belonging to either intrapsychic or objective reality, but making contact with both. This space allows players to engage with complex psychological material-such as compulsions, self-and-other relations, morality, and personal growth-in vivo during the play session. Through exploration of the structural building blocks of games and specific case examples, the author suggests that an understanding of how and why patients play games can serve as an important clinical tool for sparking therapeutic change.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fostering Change in Personality Configurations

Anaclitic and Introjective Pgtients in Psychoanalysis

Andrzej Werbart PHD and Sonja Levander PHD

Sonja Levander

SE-106 91 Stockholm, Sweden. E-mail: andrzej.werbart@psychology.su.se and andrzej@werbart.se

Stockholm, Sweden

Sonja Levander  

personality configurations, relatedness and self-definition, psychoanalysis, prototype-matching, patient perspective

Treatment goals in psychoanalysis often include changes in underlying psychological structures. Different patterns of change have been demonstrated in anaclitic and introjective patients. In a series of 14 cases, we took a further step and examined changes in the anaclitic–introjective personality configurations following psychoanalysis, as well as patients' experiences of these changes. Clinical case formulations were based on repeated interviews and the application of prototype-matching methodology. Patients' experiences were explored by applying inductive thematic analysis. We found a development toward more mature levels of differentiation and individuation in the anaclitic group, whereas relatedness and intimacy remained problematic in some introjective cases. Patients described their experienced changes in terms of complementary personality configuration, but the introjective group described more benefits from psychoanalysis. Several patients expressed their ambivalence toward these changes and a feeling of loss of their former selves. Patients' view of their analysts and the analytic method were congruent with patients' primary personality configuration. To reactivate developmental processes, the psychoanalytic technique has to be adjusted to the anaclitic and introjective patients' different needs and defenses.

This article was published Online First September 21, 2015.

Andrzej Werbart, PhD, Department of Psychology, Stockholm University; Sonja Levander, PhD, Stockholm, Sweden.

Sonja Levander was at Stockholm Centre of Public Health, Unit of Mental Health, Stockholm, Sweden.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Necessity as a Nightmare or as a Pathway to Freedom:

Freud's Dilemma, a Human Dilemma

Wayne Viney PHD and Elizabeth Parker BS

Elizabeth Parker

200 W. Lake Street, Fort Collins, CO, 80523-1876. E-mail: wwviney@comcast.net

Elizabeth Parker  

causality, choice, determinism, free will, Freud

Contemporary interest in the neurosciences and cognitive studies has revitalized debates about the meanings and implications of free will and determinism. Dilemmas associated with the various positions remain relevant to a broad range of psychological theories and practices as well as to legal, moral, political, and religious beliefs and practices. Workable solutions to the issue remain as vexing and elusive as ever, partly because to date there is no concept of free will or determinism that does not do violence to some of the dimensions of ordinary human experience. For example, does a law-and-order, hard-wired, completely determined worldview foreclose on the possibility of any genuinely open future? On the other hand, are there self-generated autonomous activities entirely, or in any sense, free from material, formal, and social influences? This paper focuses on Freud's extensive but little-known struggles with the issue, particularly in the early years of his practice. Examining his work with fresh eyes uncovers intellectual minefields and meanings of freedom highly provocative, informative, and relevant to 21st century debates.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shrink: A Cultural History of Psychoanalysis in America, by Lawrence R. Samuel, Lincoln, NE: University of Nebraska Press, 2013, 253 pp., $34.95

John Burnham PHD

John Burnham

106 Dulles Hall, 230 West 17th Avenue, Columbus, OH 43210. E-mail: burnham.2@osu.edu

John Burnham  

The author of this book holds a doctoral degree in American studies and serves business firms as a consultant in the field of cultural trends. He has published books on American culture, on 20th-century TV advertising, on the New York World's Fair of 1964-1965, on American wealth, on the American dream, and on other topics, including Freud on Madison Avenue: Motivation Research and Subliminal Advertising in America (2011). In Shrink, he has written a chronological account of what a general, well-read American might have run across in magazines and newspapers, especially The New York Times, concerning Freud and psychoanalysis from the 1920s to the 21st century.

This book has two problems. First, it appears to have been written in haste, with too many errors and without systematically integrating previous scholarly literature, even though the author at the beginning carefully calls attention to major secondary sources. Second, the author appears to have no more than a general or lay or journalistic understanding of psychoanalytic history, teachings, and procedures. He lumps shock therapies and drug therapies together. He thinks conservatism explains the rise of psychoanalytic “orthodoxy” in the mid-20th century, and does not mention ego psychology by name.

Indeed, one can go on listing errors and misperceptions in this book, many reproduced from writings of journalists of the past. Yet it behooves one to see that Shrink may nevertheless have substantial value as one takes into account what Samuel has accomplished. In his introduction, he disavows writing “another analytical, systematic treatise” (p. x). He instead presents a running account of what a relatively naïve reader could have read in ordinary popular, but respectable, U.S. publications. Moreover, Samuel intends his book to be read as a narrative of the ups and downs, claims and criticism, of Freud and his teachings over almost a century as both psychoanalysis and American culture changed. In fact, the author has a keen eye for the telling or colorful phrase, and the panorama of ideas and opinions that this accomplished writer presents makes for a pleasant reading experience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Critical Psychology of the Postcolonial: The Mind of Apartheid, by Derek Hook, London, UK: Routledge, 2012, 223 pp., $45.00 (paper bound)

Mrinalini Greedharry PHD

Mrinalini Greedharry

Sudbury, Ontario, Canada, P3E 2C6. E-mail: mgreedharry@laurentian.ca

Mrinalini Greedharry  

Those who have been subject to racial and colonial trauma deserve a considered and thoughtful analysis of their condition, not only to acknowledge the constitutive role colonialism has had in making modern subjects, but, hopefully, to allow them to go on living through and past colonialism. Derek Hook's book is a serious and sustained attempt to think about the possibilities of psychopolitical critique that might do this. It largely succeeds in its task of demonstrating that because the psyche and sociopolitical are produced through their relations with each other, social psychology needs to be able to give a better account of racism as both embodied and affective.

As Hook notes, positivist psychology has long neglected matters of race and racism, but the book is intended primarily as a contribution that can open up conversations with those who already take a critical and discursive approach to social psychology. The ground Hook chooses to stage this contribution upon is the strange terrain between postcolonial theory and psychoanalytic theory, not only because these fields have, respectively, already addressed many of the questions Hook pursues (though often not in conversation with each other), but also because it is by triangulating what is missing in these two fields with psychology that he believes he can provide a means to work us past the impasse of whether race and racism are, to paraphrase Paul Gilroy, a pre- or postpolitical phenomenon.

Even in its most general outline the project is an ambitious one, but the scope of that ambition becomes almost overwhelming as we are moved through the individual chapters on vernacular social psychology, abjection, psychoanalytic postcolonial theory, colonial stereotypes and the “real” of the racialized body.

Members of Underrepresented Groups: Reviewers for Journal Manuscripts Wanted

If you are interested in reviewing manuscripts for APA journals, the APA Publications and Communications Board would like to invite your participation. Manuscript reviewers are vital to the publications process. As a reviewer, you would gain valuable experience in publishing. The P&C Board is particularly interested in encouraging members of underrepresented groups to participate more in this process.

If you are interested in reviewing manuscripts, please write APA Journals at Reviewers@apa.org. Please note the following important points:

To be selected as a reviewer, you must have published articles in peer-reviewed journals. The experience of publishing provides a reviewer with the basis for preparing a thorough, objective review.

To be selected, it is critical to be a regular reader of the five to six empirical journals that are most central to the area or journal for which you would like to review. Current knowledge of recently published research provides a reviewer with the knowledge base to evaluate a new submission within the context of existing research.

To select the appropriate reviewers for each manuscript, the editor needs detailed information. Please include with your letter your vita. In the letter, please identify which APA journal(s) you are interested in, and describe your area of expertise. Be as specific as possible. For example, “social psychology” is not sufficient—you would need to specify “social cognition” or “attitude change” as well.

Reviewing a manuscript takes time (1-4 hours per manuscript reviewed). If you are selected to review a manuscript, be prepared to invest the necessary time to evaluate the manuscript thoroughly.

APA now has an online video course that provides guidance in reviewing manuscripts. To learn more about the course and to access the video, visit http://www.apa.org/pubs/authors/review-manuscript-ce-video.aspx.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Testing That Matters: Creating a Road Map for Effective Treatment, by Anthony D. Bram and Mary Jo Peebles. Washington, DC: American Psychological Association, 2014, 468 pp. (ISBN 9781-4338-1674-1, US $79.95)

Sandra Russ PHD

Sandra Russ

Cleveland, OH 44106-7123. E-mail: swr@case.edu

Sandra Russ  

A Model for Psychological Assessment

Psychological Testing That Matters is an outstanding text for many reasons. The approach to psychological testing that Bram and Peebles take provides an excellent model for integrating test findings with a focus on treatment planning. The authors are experts in psychological assessment and are seasoned clinicians. They tackle the hard question of how to weigh the evidence and decide which conclusions can be said with confidence and which are speculative. Principles of inference-making are presented. This is a clinically sophisticated presentation that uses language that is understandable for beginning students. The focus of the book is to “explicate methods of inference-making and synthesis that answer referral questions, create an experience of the person of the patient, and affect treatment meaningfully” (p. 8).

The organization of the book is unique and makes sense conceptually. The framework is that of a psychodynamic perspective that is focused on internal processes and personality characteristics that underlie symptoms. Using a psychodynamic approach in psychological testing teaches about personality development and processes such as affect regulation, self-other differentiation, and conflicts that influence thinking and perceiving. The benefit of this approach to clinical psychology graduate students is that they learn about the subtleties of psychological processes as well as personality assessment. As the authors point out, an understanding of the person from a psychodynamic framework does not imply psychodynamic treatment. There are many examples in this book of psychological testing leading to a cognitive-behavioral approach to treatment.

One of the strengths of the book is the identification of four key psychological capacities that should be focused on in assessment. These are reality testing and reasoning, emotional regulation, experience of self and other-implications for the therapeutic alliance, and experience of self- and other-narcissistic vulnerabilities.

[[RunningHead]]

The Buried Giant: A Novel, by Kazuo Ishiguro, New York, NY: Knopf, 2015, 317 pp., $26.95 (hardcover), $15.95 (paper-bound). ISBN: 978-0307271037

Alicia Christoff PHD

Alicia Christoff

201 Johnson Chapel, Amherst College, Amherst, MA, 01002. E-mail: aliciachristoff@gmail.com

Alicia Christoff  

No matter what the British novelist Kazuo Ishiguro writes about - a painter in post-World War II Japan in An Artist of the Floating World (1986), an English butler remembering his long years of service The Remains of the Day (1989), a young woman coming of age in a dystopic version of late 1990s England in Never Let Me Go (2005) - his concerns remain the same: memory, repression, reticence, and resignation. In his novels, Ishiguro explores what we don't know about ourselves and our own pasts - or more accurately, what we can't let ourselves know about these things: what it is that we refuse to see or to understand, what we cannot tell others when we narrate the story of our lives because we've never been able to say it even to ourselves.

Character psychology motivates Ishiguro's concerns with things forgotten and things withheld, but only in part. His preoccupations are driven just as strongly by narrative form. Over and over again, Ishiguro has constructed his novels in the same way. The central character also serves as the book's first-person narrator, one who looks back over his or her own life and takes stock, describing compelling moments from the past and teasing out their significance. The narration is calm and even-toned - almost unsettlingly so. These narrators describe traumatic events, but do not quite seem to register their emotional impact; they focus on minute details of interpersonal interaction, but never acknowledge their larger implications. Ishiguro's prose is so simple and flawless, and the narrator's observations so seemingly neutral, that we assume that the account is accurate. But the narrator is not disclosing all. As we read, we realize that something is being left unsaid, that part of the story remains untold. Beneath the still surface of the narration, there is a level of significance that it is up to the reader to gather: about collaboration with fascist governments in An Artist of the Floating World and The Remains of the Day, about resignation to an abbreviated life and compliance with a futuristic brand of systematic oppression in Never Let Me Go (I am reluctant to spoil the plot of this novel, my favorite by Ishiguro). As Nathaniel Rich puts it, “Each of Kazuo Ishiguro's seven novels has a ‘buried giant’-a monstrous secret that is gradually exhumed, with unsettling consequences” (2015). Ishiguro's narrators turn out to be unreliable, but not because they are outright lying or aiming to deceive: they are unreliable because they are dishonest with themselves.

[[RunningHead]]

The Interwoven Lives of Sigmund, Anna, and W. Ernest Freud, by Daniel Benveniste. New York, NY: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2015, 668 + xvi pp., $55.25

James Anderson PHD

James Anderson

333 North Michigan Avenue, Ste, 1900, Chicago, IL 60601. E-mail: j-anderson3@northwestern.edu

James Anderson  

For anyone who has engaged with the history of psychoanalysis in the 20th century, The Interwoven Lives of Sigmund, Anna, and W. Ernest Freud holds a great fascination. Upon first hearing the title, I felt skeptical. Here we have Sigmund Freud, the founder and dominating titan of psychoanalysis. Then Anna Freud, his daughter, one of foremost leaders of the psychoanalytic movement in midcentury and an influential writer. And finally: who? W. Ernest Freud? I had never of him. And indeed he was not a major player in the history of psychoanalysis. He was Freud's eldest grandchild, the son of his daughter, Sophie Freud Halberstadt, and her husband, Max Halberstadt. (Ernest, as I will call him, began life as Ernst Wolfgang Halberstadt. While living in England he anglicized his name from Ernst to Ernest, and, with Anna Freud's encouragement, adopted Freud as his last name.) He became a psychoanalyst, the only of Freud's grandchildren to do so, and made some modest contributions, principally related to infant observation.

But the lives of these three people were indeed interwoven. Daniel Benveniste, by looking at their lives with an emphasis on the interactions of each with the other two, does much to illuminate our understanding of Sigmund Freud and Anna Freud.

Ernest was the Zelig of psychoanalysis; incredibly he was there at so many key moments. Freud's first becoming a grandparent was due to Ernest's birth. And Freud was far more involved with him than with any of his other grandchildren. Freud had two personal losses that affected him profoundly. The first was the death of his daughter Sophie (Ernest's mother), whom Freud described as his favorite daughter (p. 164). The second was the death of Sophie's son, Heinerle (Ernest's younger brother). After the latter death, Freud told Sandor Ferenczi that he suffered the first depression of his life, and he confided to Ernest Jones that, in Jones's words, this loss “killed something in him for good” (p. 61).

Freud, who, though theorizing about psychosexual development in childhood, was not known as a close observer of children, provided one memorable and influential description of a child at play. The famous “fort-da” passage tells of a child of 1 1/2 who repeatedly threw a reel with a string attached to it over the edge of his curtained cot until it disappeared into it and then pulled it back. The child was Ernest.

Alfred Margulies MD

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street, Cambridge MA 02139. E-mail: alfred-margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

It takes chutzpah to write a biography about Sigmund Freud, and Adam Phillips knows it. For one, as the young Freud-29 years and full of heroic audacity-wrote his fiancée Martha Bernays in 1885:

One intention, as a matter of fact, I have almost finished carrying out, an intention which a number of as yet unborn and unfortunate people will one day resent. Since you won't guess what kind of people I am referring to, I will tell you at once: they are my biographers. I have destroyed all my notes of the past fourteen years, as well as letters, scientific excerpts and the manuscripts of my papers … I couldn't have matured or died without worrying about who would get hold of those old papers … As for the biographers, let them worry, we have no desire to make it too easy for them. Each one of them will be right in his opinion of “The Development of the Hero,” and I am already looking forward to seeing them go astray. (p. 25)

“Let them worry” and “go astray” and feel themselves “right in his opinion:” We have been warned. But there is an even more daunting and subtle hazard for a biographer: As Freud knew, and as we analysts know, every biography is always already a deception. And so here's Phillips warning us again-and himself-about his undertaking:

So one of the first casualties of psychoanalysis, once the facts of our lives are seen as complicated in the Freudian way, is the traditional biography. After psychoanalysis all our narratives of the past-indeed, all our coherence and plausibility-are suspect. They hide more than they seek. History begins to sound like fiction, and fiction begins to sound peculiarly wishful. (p. 6)

History then is a fiction, and this is so because we only have memories of memories and constructions of constructions. That is, we are always constructing and reconstructing our past anew-there is no untouched archive of memory; the recollected past is a rewritten past. Indeed, active construction is ubiquitous even for experience we label “present” experience. Hence the neurobiologist Gerald Edelman (1989) aptly titled his book on brain and mind: “The Remembered Present.

Parental Rejection, Personality, and Depression in the Prediction of Suicidality in a Sample of Nonclinical Young Adults

Ana Sobrinho MSc , Rui Campos PHD and Ronald Holden PHD

Ronald Holden

Apartado 94, Évora, Portugal 7002-554. E-mail: rcampos@uevora.pt

Ronald Holden  

parental rejection, self-criticism, neediness, depression, suicidality

This study tested a prediction model of suicidality in a sample of young adults. Predictor variables included perceived parental rejection, self-criticism, neediness, and depression. Participants (N = 165) responded to the Depressive Experiences Questionnaire, the Inventory for Assessing Memories of Parental Rearing Behavior, the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale, and the Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised. Perceived parental rejection, personality, and depression were assessed initially at Time 1, and depression again and suicidality were assessed 5 months later at Time 2. The proposed structural equation model fit the observed data well in a sample of young adults. Parental rejection demonstrated direct and indirect relationships with suicidality, and self-criticism and neediness each had indirect associations with suicidality. Depression was directly related to suicidality. Implications for clinical practice are discussed.

Psychopathology: From Science to Clinical Practice, Edited by Louis G. Castonguay and Thomas F. Oltmanns, New York, NY: Guilford Press, 2013, 447 pp., ISBN 978-1-4625-0803-7, $55.25.

Lotte Smith-Hansen PHD

Lotte Smith-Hansen

41 Temple Street, Boston, MA 02114. E-mail: lsmithhansen@suffolk.edu

Lotte Smith-Hansen  

Psychopathology: From Science to Clinical Practice is a wonderful attempt at bridging basic science and clinical practice. The overarching framework is noble and aims at nothing less than translating findings from basic science into specific recommendations for treatment. The individual chapters are cowritten by experts in psychopathology and experts in psychotherapy, and each chapter spans an enormous range of knowledge.

In addition to bridging science and practice, the book aims at bridging theoretical orientations by focusing on general principles of change rather than theory-specific interventions. It is argued that theoretical orientations represent the most abstract level and theory-specific interventions represent the most specific level (e.g., interpretation in psychodynamic therapy, cognitive restructuring in cognitive therapy, and exposures in behavior therapy), whereas principles of change are found in between and can therefore be useful to clinicians of different theoretical backgrounds.

Another important feature of the book is its goal to go beyond Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) symptoms and diagnoses by focusing on other features typically associated with DSM disorders, for example, the fact that irritability is a key part of depression even though it is not one of the diagnostic criteria and the fact that depressed individuals often provoke anger and frustration in other people.

Thus, the book has important strengths because of the focus on science-practice integration, the attempt to bridge theoretical orientations, and the inclusion of nonsymptom factors.

At the same time, it is clear from page one that the book has its conceptual foundations in a cognitive-behavioral symptom-based framework. For example, in the introductory chapter, the editors note that “therapists should complement their diagnostic (DSM-based) evaluation with careful consideration of nonsymptomatic factors that have been empirically associated with this disorder” (p. 4) and that “some clinicians and investigators have argued that the syndromes defined in the DSM do not represent the most useful ways to think about psychological problems” (p. 12).

Poet and Psychoanalyst: New Dialogue?

Edward McCrorie PHD

Edward McCrorie

46 Chastellux Avenue, No. 3, Newport, RI 02840. E-mail: mccrorie@providence.edu

Edward McCrorie  

poetry, psychoanalysis, social crisis

Using two of his own poems, the author invites a dialogue between creative writing and psychoanalysis. Classical concepts of Freud; new clinical efforts arising out of the attacks on September 11, 2001; the pressures of climate change; and new determinism all play their roles in a growing debate about a key question: How can we best describe and deal with the causes of human action today? In responding to these challenges, the author provides insight, analysis, and more questions. Strongly tempted to give up on some conundrums, he concludes with an invitation to continue the dialogue.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Collaborative Reading of Medical Records in Psychotherapy: A Feminist Psychoanalytic Proposal about Narrative and Empowerment

Malin Fors MSc and Nancy McWilliams PHD

Nancy McWilliams

VPP Hammerfest, Sykehusveien 28, 9613 Hammerfest, Norway. E-mail: malin.fors@mac.com

Nancy McWilliams  

medical records, medical files, psychotherapy notes, feminism, empowerment

The authors explore the undertheorized question of sharing patients' medical records as part of the therapeutic process in psychoanalytic psychotherapies. They argue that, especially with more seriously disturbed patients and those with significant personality disorders, collaborative inspection of such records can strengthen the therapeutic alliance, increase mutual understanding of the patient's problems, support the patient's self-respect, and contribute to a sense of emancipation and personal empowerment. Feminist writing about the ethics of power relationships, attention to the possibilities for misuse of power in the psychoanalytic situation, clinical and anecdotal reports of the consequences of sharing medical records, and relevant empirical investigations are considered in the context of exploring numerous aspects of mutual attention to the patient's records, both current and past. Material from the treatment of a woman with borderline personality organization and a history of destructive enactments is presented in the context of theorizing the value of looking with patients at their medical files. The authors consider potential problems and limitations of collaborative record-reading and share recommendations about integrating that process with the goals of psychotherapy.

responseSet.title: Literary and Analytic Transformations of Trauma: Repetition, Revision and Rebirth in Two Stories of Raymond Carver
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Literary and Analytic Transformations of Trauma: Repetition, Revision and Rebirth in Two Stories of Raymond Carver

Sibel Halfon PHD and Lissa Weinstein PHD

Lissa Weinstein

Emniyettepe Mah., Kazım Karabekir Cad.No: 2/13, EYÜP İSTANBUL. E-mail: sibel.halfon@bilgi.edu.tr

Lissa Weinstein  

Raymond Carver, trauma, repetition, representation, transference

A linguistic analysis of two related short stories written by Raymond Carver, “The Bath” (1981) and “A Small, Good Thing” (1983), highlights the differences in their narration of the same traumatic event—the sudden death of a child. In “The Bath,” the monotony and repetition of the linguistic structures and the lack of descriptive nouns preserve the depicted trauma in its original raw form. In contrast, in “A Small, Good Thing” the emphasis on detailed descriptions, and the varied use of language transforms the brutally realistic trauma into a deeply moving and affectively rich experience. A parallel can be drawn to the psychoanalytic process, which aims to move the patient from fixed traumatic repetitions toward sublimated forms of representation within the transference. Adele Tutter (2009, 2011) has examined different versions of Carver's stories in relation to Carver's autobiography and discussed how he projected himself in the narrative. Building on Tutter's work, this article will attempt a systematic comparison of the language of these 2 stories, discussing the trajectory from traumatic repetitions to symbolization in psychoanalytic theory.

On the Need for Openness to the Humanities in Psychoanalysis

Liran Razinsky PHD

Liran Razinsky

Ramat Gan 52900, Israel. E-mail: liranr@biu.ac.il

Liran Razinsky  

psychoanalysis, humanities, university, critical humanities, psychoanalysis and the humanities

This paper argues that psychoanalysis should open itself more to dialogue with the humanities. It shows why this is the case and analyzes the forces that go against such openness. It explores the affinities of psychoanalysis and the humanities and their common interests. It begins by addressing the direct contributions of specific research in the humanities on psychoanalysis. It then looks at the more general benefits possible were there improved dialogue with the humanities and, finally, at psychoanalysis’ affinity with a specific part of the humanities, namely, its critical ethos.

[[RunningHead]]

The Question of Aims:

Psychoanalysis and the Changing Formulations of the Life Worth Living

Gideon Lev PHD

Gideon Lev

25c Neve Sha'anan, Jerusalem, Israel 93708. E-mail: giddylev@gmail.com

Gideon Lev  

Keywords: aims of psychoanalysis, history of psychoanalysis, psychoanalysis and culture, psychoanalysis and religion

This paper examines changes in the way psychoanalytic aims have been formulated over the years. I claim that these changes are, at least partly, the result of wide cultural and social processes that took place in Western society, seen here through shifts in the prevailing worldview (Weltanschauung). Following in the steps of the philosopher Charles Taylor (2007), this study describes 3 fundamental worldviews—the religious, the secular–humanist, and the postmodern—and claims that psychoanalysis adapts itself to shifts in the dominance of these perspectives. I demonstrate how, as each worldview gained ascendancy, a corresponding analytic mode appeared or gained credence, assimilating ideals of the “life worth living” as prescribed by that particular worldview and translating them into analytic aims. The modes described are classical psychoanalysis, relational theories, and a new emerging spiritually-sensitive psychoanalysis. The contributions made by each worldview to the development of psychoanalysis are discussed, as well as the ways each worldview hindered such development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Analyst is Present”

Viewing the Psychoanalytic Process as Performance Art

Alan Karbelnig PHD ABPP

Alan Karbelnig

625 Fair Oaks Avenue, Suite 270, South Pasadena, CA 91030. E-mail: amkarbelnig@gmail.com

Alan Karbelnig  

psychoanalysis, transformational encounters, intersubjectivity, humanism, hermeneutics

Since its establishment as a profession, psychoanalytic practitioners have struggled with understanding the true nature of their work. Many remain devoted to Freud's medical model (Freud, 1958) and aspire to establish a logical-positivistic basis for psychoanalysis. Others view the field more broadly, and consider psychoanalysis a distinctively humanistic discipline. This paper suggests that the bifurcation may be resolved by focusing on clinical as opposed to theoretical psychoanalysis—an emphasis that illuminates its artistic elements. Psychoanalysts’ work may be likened to performance artists, primarily because they work to create an experience in their patients. In addition, they give with their psyche-somas, reminiscent of how actors use their bodies as instruments; they face each session in a fashion akin to how painters face the white canvas or writers the blank page; and they choose from an infinite number of possible models for coconstructing ways of understanding their patients’ experiences. Regardless of past or future theoretical differences, psychoanalysts provide creative, transformative experiences most accurately described as transformational encounters. Psychoanalysis is a verb, a process. While it alleviates pain caused by various mental disorders, it also assists individuals in discovering their individuality, authenticity, and singularity. In support of the artistic foundation of the psychoanalytic process, the paper includes three scenes that demonstrate how patients experience precipitous, essential breaks in their repetitive, internal dramas, resulting in them experiencing themselves as beings, capable of change. It thereby demonstrates how psychoanalytic practitioners bring something new to life, further validating how psychoanalysis qualifies as an artistic endeavor.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapists' Self-perception, Attachment, and Relationship: The Role of Selfobject Needs

Limor Goldner PHD

Limor Goldner

199 Aba Khoushy 199 Avenue, Mount Carmel, Haifa 3498839, Israel. E-mail: limor.goldner@gmail.com

Limor Goldner  

client-therapist relationship, narcissistic pathology, therapists' characteristics, attachment security

The associations between therapists' narcissistic vulnerability as manifested in their hunger for selfobject needs, attachment orientation, self-esteem, and authenticity, along with the quality of the therapeutic relationship with their clients were explored in a sample of 200 Israeli therapists. In addition, a regression model was tested in which therapists' hunger for selfobject needs were posited to mediate the association between therapists' attachment orientation and self-perception, which in turn was expected to mediate the association between therapists' hunger for selfobject needs and the therapist-client bond. Hunger for selfobject needs was associated with anxious attachment orientation. Hunger for the selfobject need for mirroring was associated with low levels of self-esteem, authenticity, and the quality of the therapeutic bond. The therapists' needs for mirroring mediated the association between therapists' anxious attachment orientation and their self-perception, which in turn mediated the relationship between therapists' hunger for selfobject needs and the quality of the relationship. The theoretical and clinical implications for therapy are discussed.

Resurrecting Oedipus: Poussin's Birth of Bacchus

Adele Tutter MD PHD

Adele Tutter

300 Central Park West, New York, NY 10024. E-mail: atutter@mac.com

Adele Tutter  

Oedipus, Narcissus, Metamorphoses, Ovid, Nicholas Poussin

The controversial canvas, Nicolas Poussin's Birth of Bacchus, is fundamentally reconstrued as representing not only Ovid's serial narration of the myths of Bacchus and of Narcissus and Echo in The Metamorphoses, but also the history of this text. In particular, Poussin alludes to the intertextual relationship between Ovid's Narcissus and Sophocles’ Oedipus. Poussin's interpretation of classical myth points to a more complex interrelationship between dyadic and triadic dynamics in development. An interdisciplinary approach to the aesthetic interpretation of myth underscores the reciprocal value of the arts to psychoanalytic theory.

[[RunningHead]]

The Interplay between Loss and Enchantment in the works of J. R. R. Tolkien

John Rosegrant PHD

John Rosegrant

3242 Chestnut Street, New Orleans, LA 70115. E-mail: rosegrantj@gmail.com

John Rosegrant  

loss, enchantment, development, literature

In The Lord of the Rings and other writings, J. R. R. Tolkien creates enchantment for the reader while at the same moment he puts enchantment at risk for loss. Tolkien never resolves whether enchantment or loss “wins,” instead leaving them in a dialogue. This interplay between enchantment and loss symbolizes the need to maintain metaphorical enchantment (connection, meaning) while confronted by loss. One reason many adolescents are absorbed by Tolkien and other fantasists is that adolescence involves a “normal disenchantment” due to turning from play to increased engagement with adult realities. Tolkien embodied the interplay of enchantment and loss in his life. His writings are an expression of his struggle to maintain enchantment in the face of the many losses he experienced, ranging from his orphaning to the horrors of World War I.

Rebecca Chaplan MD

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F, New York, NY 10024. E-mail: rlchaplan@gmail.com

Rebecca Chaplan  

Cleaning Up

Two remarkable books were published recently that deserve the attention of psychoanalysts. Both are about working through the death of parents and cleaning up the mess left behind from an ambivalent relationship. The books could hardly be less alike: one is a graphic memoir by a cartoonist well known for getting laughs from neurotic anxiety, and the other an installment of a monumental six-volume work, whose intellectually sophisticated author has been heralded as a successor to Proust. Yet dissimilar as could be in format and tone, they share in common a commitment to truth, sharp observation, reflections on duty and guilt, and meditations on memory. In both books, memories are represented by stuff, especially the disgusting, discarded stuff that is left behind, and the job of emotional reconciliation is represented concretely by the cleaning up of grime and filth. Both authors are exploring how they have been formed and deformed by their parents. It may seem haphazard to compare them, but I think it is worth considering these two texts together because they are trying out new forms to tell the same basic story: Chast in the young genre of the serious graphic narrative and Knausgaard in an experiment with prose that questions conventions of structure and style. In that they are each self-consciously about finding a form to express and explore the process of psychic change, both books are explicitly about the process of writing and drawing as a method of cure.

Chast and Knausgaard share a diagnosis. Each has been consumed with anxiety since childhood, experienced as an always-present sense of foreboding. Each author undertakes in the memoir to describe a new sense of agency in relation to a bad parent, a process that ultimately transforms the way they are in the world. For both, the task of cleaning up is experienced as an unwelcome duty, but becomes the catalyst for inner change that brings relief and resolution.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and History

Michael Roth PHD

Michael Roth

Middletown CT 06457. E-mail: mroth@wesleyan.edu

Michael Roth  

psychoanalysis, history

The humanities and psychoanalysis both generate skepticism from a culture ever more suspicious of enterprises that cultivate a tolerance for ambiguity and a recognition of ambivalence. Neither the humanities nor psychoanalysis can thrive in an environment so reductionist that it pursues measurement at the cost of imagination. Historical consciousness, at the core of the humanities and psychoanalysis, resists this reductionism. The practice of historical inquiry, of making meaning from the past, will never eradicate ambiguity and ambivalence. This essay will explore the connections between psychoanalysis and history beginning with Freud's work, and then consider the impact of psychoanalysis on the approaches to the past taken by historians in more recent years.

[[RunningHead]]

Frankenstein, Spellbound, and World War Z

Evolving Concepts of the Unconscious

Joseph Newirth PHD

Joseph Newirth

Garden City, NY 11050. E-mail: joenewirth@gmail.com

Joseph Newirth  

unconscious, film, culture, consumer capitalism, psychoanalytic theory

The author uses popular movies to explore changing concepts of the unconscious. He argues that psychoanalysis has ignored cultural influences on the unconscious and emphasizes the changes in socioeconomic conditions from the agrarian cultures of the past to the contemporary socioeconomic culture of consumer capitalism. Three films are used to demonstrate this argument: Frankenstein, Spellbound, and World War Z. Frankenstein represents the unconscious as uncontrollable impulses projected onto the other, reflecting the shift from an agrarian to an industrial socioeconomic system that undermined fixed social roles and emphasized the accumulation of capital. Spellbound represents the unconscious during the post-World War II period of optimism and economic expansion, when the unconscious became thought of as confusion between self and other, good and evil. World War Z represents a contemporary view of the unconscious in which individuals struggle with impersonal, unsatisfied hunger caused by their commodification in consumer capitalism and the resulting apocalyptic worldview.

Within the Confines of Character: A Review of Suicidal Behavior and Personality Style

Katie Lewis PHD , Kevin Meehan PHD , Nicole Cain PHD and Philip Wong PHD

Philip Wong

25 Hackett Blvd., MC 164, Albany NY 12208. E-mail: lewisk2@mail.amc.edu

Brooklyn

Philip Wong  

suicide, personality, interpersonal-psychological theory, escape theory, cognitive-affective processing

Suicide is estimated to be 1 of the top 5 leading causes of death for individuals between the ages of 5 and 44. Although the majority of empirical research on suicide has focused on specific risk factors, this has not led to improvement in the prediction of suicide in specific individuals. Contemporary theories such as the Interpersonal-Psychological or Escape theory of suicide have provided promising models of self-destructive behavior, improving on risk factor research by focusing on the connections between precipitating stressors and individual personality-based processes. However, these theories remain limited because of their assumption that all suicidal individuals experience similar vulnerabilities to the same sources of psychological trauma (e.g., interpersonal isolation or experiences of failure and shame). This review argues for an alternative personality-based conceptualization of suicide that contextualizes risk factors within broader models of personality, including considerations of personality-based cognitive and affective processes such as primary motivating psychological needs, attentional biases, and capacities for impulse control. The integration of contemporary personality theory with new and developing methodological approaches to suicide research will aim toward a more sensitive and comprehensive understanding of suicidal behavior in specific individuals, and greater accuracy in prediction of risk at the clinical assessment level.

[[RunningHead]]

Agency, Surrender, and Grace in Psychoanalysis

Jeremy Safran PHD

Jeremy Safran

80 Fifth Avenue, 7th Floor, New York, NY, 10011. E-mail: SafranJ@newschool.edu

Jeremy Safran  

agency, will, narcissism, surrender, grace

There is a vital dialectic between agency and surrender in life and in the analytic process. Without an ability to will, one cannot choose one's actions; one becomes a passive victim of circumstances rather than an agent who can influence one's own destiny. On the other hand, an exaggerated sense of agency fails to take into account the limits of our ability to control life, and is associated with a type of narcissistic omnipotence that can be linked with an experience of isolation. In this article I explore some of the subtleties of the interplay between willing and surrendering in the analytic process. I also examine the way in which an inability to surrender can impede the patient's ability to take in what the analyst has to offer. And finally I adapt the concept of grace from theological discourse to highlight a dimension of the analytic process that involves an emergence of the patient's capacity to make constructive use of the analyst's interventions.

[[RunningHead]]

Reconsidering Resistance

Joan Lentz PHD

Joan Lentz

825 Nicollet Mall, Suite 845 Minneapolis, Minnesota 55402. E-mail: joanlentz@aol.com

Joan Lentz  

resistance, self-sufficiency, dependence, free association

Resistance can be identified in the treatment when the patient attempts to take over the resolution of his problems in regressed, but familiar ways. Long regarded as opposition-against the treatment and against the authority of the analyst-resistance has evoked images of battle and has been a source of frustration for both analyst and patient. Modern theorists view the resistant patient as trying his best and entreat analysts to keep in mind the pain behind the patient's resistance. But impatience prevails when a resistance persists. This paper portrays a form of resistance that appears in the treatment as self-sufficient behavior. Faced with fears of entering territory that is unknown, inevitably alone and unable to depend, it is not surprising that our patient eschews our “help” in favor of known, albeit inadequate, solutions. Recognizing its appearance and explaining this framework to our patients may help mitigate a resistance.
